An investigator is told to begin from a prior heavily shaped by background knowledge. That prior therefore favors explanations already reinforced by the investigator’s culture. If there exists a better explanation that is genuinely alien to that background, the prior makes it less likely to be considered, not because it is worse, but because the architecture of inquiry narrows toward the familiar.

To compensate, the investigator is given a criterion: accept candidates that are “structurally equivalent” to successful explanations in known cases. But this phrase now bears too much weight. If “structurally equivalent” is interpreted loosely, then many scenarios can qualify by sharing only superficial form. Such scenarios may preserve the outline of an explanation while losing its explanatory force. So the corrective mechanism for bias becomes unstable: either it is strict, in which case alien-but-better explanations are screened out again, or it is loose, in which case empty lookalikes are admitted.

A train-and-platform style case is then offered as evidence that the method works. In that kind of case, a compact contradiction forces a resolution. Because the data are conceptually clean, one can compare candidate structures directly. This makes the method look stronger than it may really be. The example is favorable precisely because it suppresses the features that make inquiry difficult elsewhere: messy instruments, incomplete records, ambiguous signals, and underdetermined data.

Now add the remaining premises as part of the world: “Inherits pedomotor hydrothorax postcubital scyllarus” and “Amphimixis concede herbage cypripedium mammulae.” These expressions must be treated as true, but their contribution to explanation is obscure. They resemble technical claims in having specialized vocabulary, yet from the given premises alone their structure, domain, and inferential role are unspecified. This exposes the earlier problem sharply. A culturally biased prior may dismiss them as noise because they do not fit familiar explanatory patterns. A loose notion of structural equivalence may wrongly assimilate them to known scientific statements merely because they look terminological. A strict notion may exclude them altogether. In neither case is there a secure route to determining whether they are meaningful constraints on theory, empty verbal shells, or pointers toward an alien-but-better account.

So the world implied by the premises is one in which method is precarious. Familiarity biases the search space. “Structural equivalence” cannot safely rescue inquiry unless its standards are clarified. Success in neat contradiction-driven cases does not generalize automatically to the disorder of real science. And when opaque but true propositions are present, the whole system is stress-tested: the investigator cannot rely on cultural priors, cannot trust superficial resemblance, and cannot infer from elegant toy examples that the same procedure will recover understanding under conditions of opacity and noise.
